States may not simply attack other states. Period.

The attack by the United States and Israel on Iran is an act of aggression that undermines international law,
and thus the last vestige of credibility of the so-called "rules-based world order."
This cannot be dismissed as an unfortunate incident or a necessary evil.

This is a deliberate and serious violation of fundamental principles
intended precisely to protect states from indiscriminate and unilateral violence.

The context in which this attack took place is particularly poignant. While negotiations with Iran were underway in Geneva,
brokered by Oman, diplomacy proved to be little more than a necessary prelude to war.
Talks intended to build trust and prevent escalation were used to mislead Iran.

When negotiations are used to mislead a state and
pave the way for military violence—even with the explicit aim of killing the head of state—
then not only Iran is the victim, but diplomacy as well.
Under such circumstances, any future consultation loses its credibility and meaning.

Iran must also stop*?!?*
Proponents of military intervention often point to the nature of the Iranian regime.
But that argument is legally and fundamentally irrelevant. International law makes no distinction between "good" and
"bad" governments when it comes to the prohibition of aggression.

If we justify kidnapping or assassinating heads of state,
we open the door to permanent lawlessness.
Nor is the belligerent nature of the Israeli government or Trump's relevant
to this assessment. States may not simply attack other states. Period.
There was no acute self-defense and no imminent threat that could justify a
preemptive strike, nor was there authorization from the UN Security Council.

For the same reason, Iran must also immediately stop attacking Arab countries in the region that are not involved in the attack on Iran.
?!? NB! US bases in those countries, not the country itself.

Permanent Lawlessness
At the same time, it is entirely understandable that many Iranians who suffered under this regime—
including many Iranian Dutch citizens—experience feelings of relief and joy at the death of Khamenei and other high-ranking officials.
These personal traumas, resulting from decades of oppression, deserve full recognition.

But this understanding is separate from the question of whether the actions of the United States and Israel are permissible.
Moreover, the experiences of Iranians must not—as was the case previously during the war with Iraq—
be used as a moral alibi for violations of international law. Recognition of past injustices can never serve as justification for new violations of law.

Precisely for this reason, Trump's public statements are all the more disturbing. He explicitly states that the goal is to overthrow the Iranian government. Regime change, however, is not a legal basis for the use of force. If we justify the kidnapping or assassination of heads of state because we find their policies or ideology reprehensible, we open the door to permanent lawlessness.

And there are countless leaders – including democratically elected ones – who could be targeted on similar grounds.

“Bloodthirsty thugs” - interesting word choice.
THUG comes from India - the Hindi word ṭhag (ठग). The British then used “Thuggee laws” to criminalize entire innocent tribes and communities under colonial rule. The Criminal Tribes Act of 1871 literally branded people “born criminals” by birth - nomadic communities, lower castes, the marginalized. Colonial control disguised as law enforcement.
Then the word traveled to America, where it evolved into racially charged slang used to dehumanize people.
And now here it is - deployed by a sitting president to demonize an entire nation’s leadership.
A word stolen from colonized people, used to justify colonization, then weaponized in America, now used on a world stage to escalate geopolitical tension.
The irony is loud.
India got independence in 1947. These tribes were finally decriminalized in 1952. The word that was used to oppress them never got decolonized - it just found new targets.
Words carry history. This one carries centuries of it. 🔥
